A novel single-stage pre-training method, termed M3I Pre-training, is proposed within the framework.
A generic pre-training theoretical framework is introduced within the larger framework that covers existing mainstream pre-training methods.
The framework aims to maximize mutual information between input representation and target representation as a key goal.
The framework includes a prediction term along with a regularization term to derive a prediction term reconstructing training targets from network inputs.
The framework incorporates a regularization term that requires the distribution of the target to maintain high entropy to avoid collapse.
The framework is designed to include existing pre-training approaches by adopting different forms of input-target paired data and their representations.
The framework provides possible directions to design an all-in-one pre-training method for large-scale pre-training with various data sources.

edge: Two masked views from two different images are used as input. The representation of one image is used to reconstruct the same view, while the other image is used to reconstruct a different augmented view.
Both representations will predict their corresponding annotated category or paired texts.

In this way, a novel pre-training approach called M3I Pre-training is proposed, which can combine the merits of supervised/weakly-supervised/self-supervised pre-training and enables large-scale vision foundation models to benefit from multi-modal/source large-scale data.
All existing mainstream pre-training methods share a common optimization objective, i.e., maximizing the mutual information between input and target representation.
The mutual information pre-training framework is extended to a multi-input multi-target setting to gather the benefit of various pre-training supervision signals.
A novel single-stage pre-training approach called M3I Pre-training is proposed to gather the benefit of various pre-training supervision signals.
Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach.
The approach is used in a multi-target setting as demonstrated in comprehensive experiments.
The approach includes pre-training the model InternImage-H with billion-level parameters.
The pre-trained model InternImage-H sets a new record on basic detection and segmentation tasks.
The model InternImage-H has billion-level parameters.
The model performs well on basic detection tasks.
The model sets a new record on segmentation tasks.
The model achieves 65.4 box AP on COCO test-dev for detection tasks.
The model achieves 62.9 mIoU on ADE20K for segmentation tasks.

Self-supervised Pre-training has become popular in recent years.
Self-supervised Pre-training does not require annotations.
Self-supervised Pre-training enables the usage of large-scale unlabeled data.
Self-supervised Pre-training can be divided into two kinds of methods.
Intra-view tasks are a kind of method within Self-Supervised Pre-training (SSP) where input and target are created from the same view.
Inter-view tasks are another kind of method within Self-Supervised Pre-training (SSP) where different augmented views are adopted as input and target.
Intra-view tasks include methods like auto-encoder which is used for creating input and target from the same view.
Inter-view tasks involve methods like dense/global instance discrimination where different augmented views are used as input and target.
Methods within dense/global instance discrimination like contrastive learning are used for instance discrimination.

M3I Pre-training combines different supervision signals in a single stage, avoiding the problems of multi-stage pre-training.
The goal of pre-training is to learn representations that can well represent the training samples.

The input training data x and target training data y are extracted from s by some transform operations.
The transform operations t are typically data-irrelevant, e.g., 'apply image augmentation', 'get annotated category', or 'get paired text'.
In vision-centric pre-training, the input data x is usually an augmented image.

Multi-input Pre-training uses multiple inputs with one target for each input.
Multi-input Pre-training (N = M) uses multiple inputs i=1, Y = {yi}N with one target for each input X = {xi}N i=1, where yi is the corresponding sampled target of xi.

multi-input pre-training is widely used in supervised pre-training (typically N = 2), where different images are mixed through Mixup [94] or CutMix [90]. It has proven to be critical for improving accuracy and providing a more stable generalization ability.

Multi-target Pre-training (N = 1) only uses multiple targets for the same input as X = x, Y = {yi}M i=1. Some previous works have explored the use of multiple targets [39, 44, 59, 89].

Some previous works have explored the use of multiple targets [39, 44, 59, 89]. One line of research tries to combine weakly-supervised pre-training with specific forms of self-supervised pre-training, such as MaskCLIP [23] and FLAVA [59].

Some previous works have explored the use of multiple targets [39, 44, 59, 89]. Another line studies the combination of supervised pre-training and self-supervised pre-training, such as SupCon [39] or SupMAE [44]. These methods display the effectiveness of multiple targets.

Comparison between M3I Pre-training and single-input single-target pre-training methods shows that M3I Pre-training w/o mix can achieve superior or comparable results on all tasks, especially on LVIS and ADE20k benchmarks.
M3I Pre-training w/o mix compared with single-input single-target pre-training methods demonstrates its superior or comparable results on various tasks, particularly on LVIS and ADE20k benchmarks.

M3I Pre-training w/o mix shows high results on LVIS and ADE20k datasets due to Image Classification pre-training focusing on better semantic information, essential for understanding long-tail classes.
M3I Pre-training w/o mix benefits from Intra-view SSP's excellence in spatial sensitivity, leading to good performance on the COCO dataset.
M3I Pre-training w/o mix leverages the benefits of M3I Pre-training by combining multiple pre-training methods effectively, resulting in better performances on all tasks.
